content:
First we index the non‐multiples of 3 in “blocks” of two:

Block j contains the two numbers  
 3j − 2, 3j − 1,  
so that  
 M₁=1, M₂=2; M₃=4, M₄=5; M₅=7, M₆=8; …  
In general  
 M_{2j−1}=3j−2, M_{2j}=3j−1.

We seek 2n consecutive terms of M whose sum is 300.  There are two cases for the starting index k:

Case 1: k=2m−1 is odd.  Then the 2n terms run from block j=m up to j=m+n−1, each block fully included.  Their sum is  
 S = ∑_{j=m}^{m+n−1}[(3j−2)+(3j−1)]  
  = ∑_{j=m}^{m+n−1}(6j−3)  
  = 3n(2m+n−2).  
Setting S=300 gives  
 n(2m+n−2)=100.  

Case 2: k=2m is even.  Then the sequence is the second element of block m, both elements of blocks m+1 … m+n−1, and the first element of block m+n.  One finds  
 S=3n(2m+n−1)=300,  
i.e. n(2m+n−1)=100.

Since n must divide 100, one tests n∈{1,2,4,5,10,20,25,50,100}, and checks which make m=(…)/2 a positive integer.  The only solutions are

Case 1: (n,m)=(2,25) or (10,1),  
Case 2: (n,m)=(1,50),(4,11),(5,8).

Hence the possible values of n are

\boxed{1,2,4,5,10}.

First we index the non‐multiples of 3 in “blocks” of two:

Block j contains the two numbers  
 3j − 2, 3j − 1,  
so that  
 M₁=1, M₂=2; M₃=4, M₄=5; M₅=7, M₆=8; …  
In general  
 M_{2j−1}=3j−2, M_{2j}=3j−1.

We seek 2n consecutive terms of M whose sum is 300.  There are two cases for the starting index k:

Case 1: k=2m−1 is odd.  Then the 2n terms run from block j=m up to j=m+n−1, each block fully included.  Their sum is  
 S = ∑_{j=m}^{m+n−1}[(3j−2)+(3j−1)]  
  = ∑_{j=m}^{m+n−1}(6j−3)  
  = 3n(2m+n−2).  
Setting S=300 gives  
 n(2m+n−2)=100.  

Case 2: k=2m is even.  Then the sequence is the second element of block m, both elements of blocks m+1 … m+n−1, and the first element of block m+n.  One finds  
 S=3n(2m+n−1)=300,  
i.e. n(2m+n−1)=100.

Since n must divide 100, one tests n∈{1,2,4,5,10,20,25,50,100}, and checks which make m=(…)/2 a positive integer.  The only solutions are

Case 1: (n,m)=(2,25) or (10,1),  
Case 2: (n,m)=(1,50),(4,11),(5,8).

Hence the possible values of n are

\boxed{1,2,4,5,10}.
